sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit for The Forge

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Complaint & incident escalation** – member injury reports, staff conflicts, billing disputes, safeguarding concerns; no documented triage or resolution pathway
- **Member retention/churn intervention** – no SOP for identifying at-risk members, win-back campaigns, or exit interviews
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no documented process for daily cash/card reconciliation, refund authorization, or audit trails
- **Onboarding (staff)** – 17 coach SOPs exist but no unified staff induction covering H&S, facilities access, payroll, confidentiality, brand standards
- **Health & safety incident response** – no documented protocol for accidents, near-misses, evacuation, defibrillator use, or statutory reporting
- **Uniform/branding standards** – no SOP on coach appearance, member-facing communication tone, or brand consistency audits
- **Class cancellation & substitute protocol** – who covers, notification timeline, member communication
- **Data protection & GDPR compliance** – no documented process for member data requests, consent management, or deletion
- **Member complaints resolution** – distinct from incidents; covers service quality, coach conduct, facility issues

**Needs updating**

- **Marketing SOPs (10 of 12)** – Email Newsletter and Blog Post missing; likely also missing: social media posting cadence, content calendar planning, member referral incentives, paid ad approval workflow
- **Client onboarding** – 22 SOPs exist but review for: first-class experience consistency, fitness assessment documentation, goal-setting framework, follow-up touchpoints at day 3, week 2, month 1
- **Coaching standards** – likely missing: class observation/quality audit process, member feedback collection, performance review cadence, continuing education sign-off
- **Facilities**

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE — URGENT PRIORITY

**Biggest Problem: June Revenue Down 13% vs May (£20,199 → £17,711), Active Clients UP 20% (122 → 147) but AR/M Collapsed 27% (£165.56 → £120.48).**

- **Pricing strategy failure.** You've acquired 25 new members in two months but your Average Revenue per Member has fallen hard. Either new joiners are on discounted intro rates or you're selling lower-tier packages. With the larger studio now operational, raise base membership price by 8–12% immediately (benchmark typical London boutique fitness at £60–75/month; if you're under £50, you're leaving money on table). Protect existing members with 3-month grandfather clause.

- **Upsell discipline missing.** At 147 active members, you should be selling PT sessions, nutrition coaching, or class packages (e.g. "Unlimited+" tier at +£15/month). No data on secondary revenue. Introduce a simple add-on offer at renewal: 4-week PT starter pack (4 × 30min sessions, £120–150). Target 15% uptake = +£2,700/month.

- **Attrition is volatile (May 5.74%, June 0%):** June zero churn looks like data lag or seasonal quiet. Assume 3–5% run-rate going forward. At 147 members and 5% churn, you're losing ~7 members/month. Implement exit survey (2 questions, 20 seconds) to catch payment issues or class scheduling mismatches before cancellation.

---

## EXPENSES — STRUCTURAL ISSUE POST-RELOCATION

**Trend: Non-People Expenses Erratic & High. May £9,233 → June £9,390 (stable but elevated vs Sep–Nov average £4,573).**

- **Non-People costs 53% of revenue in June (£9,390 ÷ £17,711).** Pre-relocation (Sep–Nov), they averaged 31%. Bigger studio means higher rent/utilities/insurance, but £9k/month suggests setup/transition bills still embedded. Audit June invoice stack: identify one-time items (IT setup, equipment delivery, lease deposit, signage). Separate recurring baseline from relocation tail costs. By August, non-people should stabilize at £6,500–7,000 max (38–40% of revenue).

- **People costs stable & efficient** at £3,686 (21% of revenue in

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her Build: Lower (17 sessions), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo should take most Build classes (her strength); Eilis can absorb Elevate and Strongevity to develop range and confidence before potentially moving to Tuesdays later.

• **Move Grace's PT and InBody load:** Her 9 InBody scans are admin—assign to whoever's available. Her 3 No Sweat Intros should go to Eilis (member retention / relationship-building) or JoJo depending on member preference.

• **Reduce JoJo's Personal Training load:** At 44 PT sessions, JoJo is stretched thin. She's also the only coach doing Tuesdays (highest demand/technical day). Shift some PT to Eilis to free JoJo for class delivery and sustainable capacity.

• **Plug the Circuits gap:** Grace was taking 0 Circuits; JoJo only does 6. If Circuits is popular, Eilis should build this into her rotation—it's lower-skill and good for her development.

• **Rebalance overall:** Aim for JoJo around 100–110 sessions (classes + essential PT), Eilis around 55–65 (growing her range), and Grace at zero. This removes founder burnout and creates resilience if someone is sick.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time between sessions:**

1. Open 30-minute "drop-in strength finisher" slots on Thursday and Monday evenings (your quietest days) — members can book a bay to finish a workout unsupervised or with a coach on standby, charged at £5-8 per slot to capture otherwise dead time.

2. Run a monthly Saturday "Barbell Clinic" workshop (60 min) targeting Barbelle and Strongevity members specifically — teach technique, progressions, and programming so these niche classes feel less intimidating and boost confidence for return visits.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. Shift Engine and Capacity to Wednesday or Friday mornings (your peak attendance days) instead of their current slots — members already showing up for Build classes are likeliest to try conditioning work if it's convenient, and you'll hit higher absolute numbers even with the same class quality.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**1. Start with your ACTUAL revenue reality — not what you think you offer**
Pull last 3 months of transactions. What are members actually *buying*? (Classes, PT sessions, nutrition add-ons, challenges?) Build your menu from what's selling, then fill gaps. Don't guess.

**2. Coach-facing menu = operational clarity, not marketing fluff**
List every service with: exact duration, required equipment, coach prep time, max capacity, any prerequisites (e.g., "PT clients only" or "beginner-friendly"). This stops coaches improvising and stops you losing money on underpriced services. Include grey areas you're currently confused about.

**3. Member menu = transparent pricing + friction removal**
Use simple tiers: **Foundation** (standard membership), **Plus** (classes + limited PT), **Premium** (unlimited PT). Price ruthlessly — if you're undercharging, fix it now. Show what's *included* vs *add-on* in a one-page PDF. Test this on 5 existing members; if they ask "wait, I get that included?" you've found hidden value to communicate.

**4. Create ONE source of truth, printed + digital**
Wall poster in the gym + Google Business profile + Instagram link in bio. Different members consume info differently; same content, multiple doors. Update quarterly minimum.

**5. Use this to identify your pricing gaps immediately**
Once it's documented, you'll see: Are you charging separately for things that should bundle? Are some services priced too low relative to effort? This is a profit fix, not just a menu.

What's your current highest-margin service?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**Immediate Actions:**

• **Audit your current drop-off points** — Pull analytics from your landing pages RIGHT NOW. Where do visitors bail? (Sign-up form? Pricing section? Hero section?) Focus refinement only on pages bleeding traffic, not perfectionism across all pages.

• **Interview recent joiners + lapsed prospects** — Call 5-10 women who signed up in the last month and ask "What almost stopped you?" Then call 5 who visited but didn't convert and ask "What was unclear?" Your FAQ answers live in these conversations, not in your head.

• **Lead with specificity, not benefits** — Don't say "Transform your fitness." Say "Beginner-friendly strength training in a women-only space with zero judgement + childcare available." Women Google "women's gym Belfast" or "strength training without men watching" — match that language exactly on your hero section.

• **Create a visible FAQ section addressing objections in order of frequency** — Include: "Will I be the weakest person here?" "Do I need experience?" "How is this different from [competitor gym]?" Test answer clarity by showing drafts to 2-3 members and timing how long it takes them to understand.

• **Set a specific conversion target** — Measure current landing page conversion rate (signups/visitors). Aim to increase by 15-20% within 4 weeks. A/B test one element at a time: headline, CTA button color, FAQ placement. Only scale what wins.

What's your current landing page conversion rate?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System for The Forge

• **Start stupidly small — one touchpoint only.** Add a 60-second QR code form after the highest-attendance class each week (not monthly — too long to wait for data). Ask 3 questions max: "What worked today?", "What frustrated you?", "Would you recommend us?" Completion is your KPI, not perfection. Monthly is dead time for a growing gym.

• **Route feedback directly to action, not a spreadsheet graveyard.** Assign one team member (probably a trainer they trust) to read responses daily and implement ONE small fix per week visibly — adjust class timing, swap music, fix a booking issue. Tell members what you changed because of them. Builds cult loyalty.

• **Bypass the "form fatigue" trap with conversation.** Your real gold is post-class check-ins — 30 seconds, one trainer asks "Anything we should know?" while members leave. Frame it as care, not data collection. You'll hear friction points they'd never type.

• **Use feedback as member acquisition bait.** Feature micro-wins in your next email/Instagram: "Members asked for earlier classes — now running 6am Mondays." People join gyms that actually listen. Make this visible monthly.

• **Set a completion target of 30% within 8 weeks.** If you're below that, your system is too friction-heavy. Kill it and simplify.

What's your highest-attendance class right now?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Map the actual member journey for EACH service first (not the service itself)**
Before writing a single SOP, spend 2 hours shadowing/interviewing: What does a new membership member actually do on day one? Week one? Where do they get stuck or ask questions? Do this for each service. Your SOPs should eliminate the top 3 friction points per service—not just document procedures.

**2. Create one master template, then customize ruthlessly**
Build ONE template (Onboarding → Usage → Troubleshooting → Escalation) and use it for all five. But customize the middle sections hard: Fuel Forward's SOP should focus on ordering/delivery logistics; Well75 on booking/rescheduling; Strongevity on progression tracking. Same structure, completely different guts.

**3. Assign one staff member as "owner" per service, not rotating writers**
Your personal training lead writes the PT SOP. Your nutrition person owns Fuel Forward. Ownership means they've lived it, they'll catch gaps, and they'll keep it updated. Each SOP takes 90 mins max if the owner actually does it—not a 5-person committee project.

**4. Test and iterate with 3 real members from each cohort**
Have a new membership member, PT client, and Fuel Forward user actually USE the SOP and time how long it takes them to get answers. Note every "I didn't understand that" moment. Update immediately. One SOP revision beats endless polishing.

**5. Embed SOPs where members actually look—not in a folder**
Slack channel, WhatsApp doc, or a simple Google Site linked

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Start with batching, not daily posting** — Pick one 2-hour block monthly (first Monday works). Film 12–16 short videos at once: member transformations, 60-second workout clips, behind-the-scenes gym moments, nutritionist Q&As. Shoot them raw; phone quality is fine. This kills the "what do I post today?" paralysis.

• **Use a £20/month tool, not spreadsheets** — Get into Buffer or Later TODAY. Set up 3–4 slots weekly (e.g., Monday 7am, Wed 12pm, Fri 6pm, Sat 9am). Schedule your batch content in 30 minutes. One less thing to think about mid-week.

• **Anchor posts to member wins, not gym info** — Don't post "New squat rack arrived." Post "Sarah squatted her bodyweight for the first time — here's what changed." Tag the member, ask followers what their goal is. This drives comments and new enquiries faster than anything else.

• **Repurpose one "pillar" post 3 different ways** — If you film a trainer demonstrating a movement, cut it three ways: full clip (Reel), 15-sec teaser (Feed), carousel showing form progression (Stories). Same effort, 3x reach.

• **Add one "response slot" weekly** — Leave Friday afternoon/evening open to reply to DMs, comments, and tag relevant local Belfast businesses or wellness accounts. Takes 20 mins, builds relationships, signals you're active (algorithm boost).

**This gets live this week or it doesn't happen.** Which platform are you strongest

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the Videography Schedule with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW as a standing appointment** — Don't treat this as "we'll figure it out." Book the next 3 sessions with Jordan today (8, 16, 24 weeks out). Put it on both your calendars with 2-week prep reminders. This removes the friction that kills consistency.

• **Create a content capture brief 2 weeks before each shoot** — List 3-4 specific angles you need: class energy shot, member testimonial, equipment showcase, behind-the-scenes. Give Jordan the brief in writing so he arrives primed, not guessing. Cuts wasted footage by 60%.

• **Designate one "content day" each cycle** — Pick the same day/time (e.g., Thursday 6-8pm). Schedule it when classes are packed and energy is highest. Brief 2-3 members beforehand to be on camera and give 30-second stories. Removes the "catch people on the fly" chaos.

• **Repurpose aggressively across 8 weeks** — One shoot = 12-15 social posts minimum (reels, stories, testimonials, gym culture clips). Map out your posting calendar the week after the shoot so content doesn't rot in a folder.

• **Build the cost into monthly ops** — Figure out what 8 weeks of sessions actually costs Jordan, then divide by 8 and budget it monthly. Stops the "can we afford this?" conversation derailing consistency mid-year.

What's your current relationship with Jordan — is budget or scheduling the real constraint?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries

• **Audit the current leak first** — Pull your last 30 trial enquiries and manually track: which ones got a response within 2 hours (highest conversion), which fell through cracks, and at what touchpoint. You'll spot exactly where automation fails or where manual handoff breaks down. This takes 90 mins and makes everything else precise.

• **Create a 3-email sequence, not one blast** — Day 1 (immediate): personalized response with trial booking link + 1 specific class recommendation based on their profile. Day 4 (if no booking): "spots filling fast" social proof + testimonial video. Day 10 (if still no action): one-to-one offer or phone number to book. Automation handles timing; Grace handles the copy to sound like her, not a robot.

• **Build a simple Zapier/Make workflow** — Trial enquiry form → Auto-check if it's peak hours (if 6pm-8pm weekday, flag for Grace to call same-day) OR auto-send Day 1 email + create task in your CRM. This takes 30 mins to set up and cuts manual response time from 4 hours to 10 minutes.

• **Lock in ONE conversion metric** — Measure: % of enquiries who complete a trial within 7 days. Track it weekly. Automation only matters if it moves this number. Test small (50 enquiries) before rolling out.

What's your current response time to enquiries, and do you know your trial-to-membership conversion rate?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist Execution for The Forge

• **Start with JoJo's revenue roles first** — Map her actual daily activities (class scheduling, member check-ins, sales conversations) into 3-4 non-negotiable weekly actions. Attach ONE metric per action: new member inquiries, retention follow-ups completed, class utilization %. Document in a shared Google Sheet she can check off daily — ownership breeds accountability.

• **Build Eilis's checklist around retention & ops** — Her actions should focus on what prevents member churn (comms, class quality, facility issues). Her KPIs: member feedback response time, class feedback scores, facility maintenance tickets resolved. Keep it to 5 actions max or it becomes noise.

• **Sarah's checklist = marketing that moves needles** — Don't ask her to "run social media." Specifics: post frequency (X per week), engagement rate targets, referral form submissions, content performance reviews. Tie each action to a lead-generation outcome, not vanity metrics.

• **Document as individual ownership docs, not a team doc** — Each person gets their own page with their actions, their KPIs, the weekly review cadence (Friday 30-min catch-up?), and what "success" looks like. This prevents groupthink and makes underperformance impossible to hide.

• **Lock it in by end of week 1** — Frame it as a 3-week pilot, not permanent. This removes friction to commitment. Review together after week 3 and adjust based on what's actually trackable vs. theoretical.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Execute Your Team Vision Session — The Forge Specific

• **Set it up as a 90-minute stand-up working session, not a meeting.** Book a Thursday morning before opening. Bring printed one-pagers of your SSO and 90-day plan so people can annotate. Standing keeps energy high and prevents drift.

• **Lead with the "why it matters to them" first.** Don't start with your vision statement. Start with: "Here's what hit our member retention last quarter… here's the revenue we need to hit by March… here's what that means for your hours/team size/bonuses." Make the plan feel like *their* problem to solve, not your directive.

• **Assign ownership, not just lanes.** Don't say "marketing is Cara's thing." Say "Cara owns member acquisition targets — she decides how we hit 40 new sign-ups by end of Q1, whether that's social, referrals, or trials." Give decision-making authority, not just tasks. This is where accountability sticks.

• **Spot-check understanding on the spot.** After covering each section, ask one person: "Walk me through how what we just discussed affects your daily work." If they can't connect it, your lane clarity failed. Fix it right there.

• **Leave with written, individual commitments.** Before people leave, have each person write down one thing they'll own in the next 2 weeks tied to the plan. Collect them. Reference them in your next check-in. This kills the "I forgot what I committed to" problem.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing for The Forge

**Day 1-3: Map reality, not perception**
- Have each team member (coaches, reception, admin) log their actual hours for 3 days in a simple spreadsheet: tasks, duration, who requested it, frequency. Don't ask "are you busy?"—see it.
- Cross-reference with your scheduling system. Spot the invisible stuff: the member who always needs extra attention, the coach covering gaps, whoever handles complaints after hours.

**Day 4-7: Identify the actual bottlenecks**
- Look for tasks done by only one person (dangerous). Look for tasks taking 40% longer than they should (training gap or scope creep?). Look for people saying yes to everything (often your most loyal staff—they're drowning).
- Ask bluntly: "What takes you longest that could take someone else 20 minutes to learn?"

**Day 8-15: Reassign ruthlessly**
- Don't "balance fairly"—balance by skill + capacity. Move admin tasks OFF your strongest coach if she's running class cover. Move repetitive bookings OFF the person doing personalised programming.
- Create a 2-week handover schedule. Make it stick—set calendar blocks.

**Day 16-30: Kill inefficiency while you're at it**
- Those 3-day logs revealed time-wasters. Cut one. (Unnecessary check-in call? Outdated admin step?) Freed time = your rebalance actually works.
- Quick weekly 10-min huddle after: "Is this working? What's still stuck?" Adjust immediately—don't wait 30 days to fix it.

# Cut 10% Costs at The Forge — Execution Play

**Audit your actual spend first (do this TODAY)**
- Pull last 3 months of P&L. Don't guess. Look specifically at: utilities, equipment maintenance, staff scheduling inefficiencies, supplier contracts, and cleaning/supplies. Most gyms waste 15-20% on utilities alone through poor HVAC scheduling.

**Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW — you have leverage**
- Call your top 3 suppliers (cleaning, equipment, protein/merchandise). Tell them you're consolidating spend and need 12-15% savings or you're switching. Women's gym operators often don't push back — they do. Get it in writing.

**Cut staff hours surgically, not across the board**
- Don't furlough. Instead: shift peak-hour staffing (keep 6-9am and 5-7pm fully staffed), reduce midday coverage, cut one admin day/week. Track member satisfaction weekly — you'll know instantly if you've gone too far.

**Kill one underperforming service this month**
- Whatever's running at <60% utilization (slow class, underused amenity, etc.) — pause it. Reallocate that resource. This saves time AND money.

**Lock in a 12-month fixed utility rate immediately**
- Prices are volatile. Get a commercial fixed rate locked in before Q4. Single biggest lever for predictable cost reduction.

**What's your current gross margin %? That'll tell me if there's a faster path.**

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — The Sharp Execution

• **Pull actuals TODAY, not end-of-month.** Set a standing calendar block for the 27th of each month (3 days before close). Grab membership revenue, class pack sales, PT revenue, and churn rate while your records are fresh. Waiting until the 1st means stale data and delayed decisions.

• **Track the THREE metrics that actually move The Forge:** Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR from memberships), Customer Acquisition Cost (total marketing spend ÷ new members), and churn rate (cancellations ÷ active members). Everything else is noise. If you're tracking 12+ metrics, you're drowning.

• **Build a one-page visual dashboard, not a spreadsheet graveyard.** Use Google Sheets or Tableau with a simple bar chart (MRR trend), one line chart (churn %), and three boxes showing CAC, lifetime value, and cash position. Email this to yourself every month—forces accountability and makes patterns visible instantly.

• **Compare month-on-month AND to your target.** Not just "revenue was £X"—flag: "MRR up 8% YoY but DOWN 2% vs. last month (why?)" and "churn hit 6% (target: 4%)." The variance is where action lives, not the absolute number.

• **Schedule a 30-min "money meeting" with yourself.** Review KPIs, write 3 bullets on what's working/broken, and decide ONE change to test next month. That's the difference between tracking and growing.

What's your biggest current leak—revenue, churn, or cost?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**Get the data foundation right NOW:**
- Pull your last 12 months of membership fees, cancellations, class attendance rates, and personal training revenue by week/month. Export to a simple spreadsheet. If you don't have clean data, spend 2 hours this week fixing it—garbage in = garbage forecasts. This is non-negotiable.

**Build a 3-scenario model (not one "best guess"):**
- Create conservative (10% member churn, no new classes), base (5% churn, 2 new offerings), and aggressive (2% churn, expansion classes) revenue projections for next 12 months. Use your ACTUAL historical conversion rates and pricing. This gives you decision-making flexibility, not false certainty.

**Link forecasts to cash, not just revenue:**
- Map when money actually hits your account (memberships are predictable; personal training often isn't). Forecast your monthly operating costs against this. The gap IS your real planning constraint—not revenue.

**Set one leading indicator you'll watch weekly:**
- Pick either new member sign-ups or membership retention rate. Track it every Monday for 4 weeks. This tells you if your forecast is holding or if you need to adjust tactics *before* month-end surprises you.

**Tie this to one immediate decision:**
- Use this forecast to decide: Can you afford to hire that extra PT instructor now, or does it need to wait 3 months? Make that call this week based on the data.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge — Execution Plan

**1. Start with revenue capture (this week)**
Pull last 3 months of actual numbers — class attendance (cap × price), PT sessions booked, add-ons (supplements, merchandise, challenges). If you're not tracking add-ons granularly, start now. Don't wait for perfect data; rough accuracy beats delay. Export into a simple spreadsheet by service line.

**2. Assign costs ruthlessly (the hard part)**
For classes: allocate instructor pay + studio utilities (rent/heating split by room usage). For PT: trainer pay + any 1-on-1 facility cost. For add-ons: COGS + handling. Don't overthink overhead allocation — use a simple % of gross revenue for admin/marketing across all three. The goal is directional accuracy, not audit-ready precision.

**3. Calculate unit economics immediately**
Gross margin per class = (class revenue per session ÷ max capacity) minus instructor cost. PT margin = hourly rate minus trainer split. Add-ons margin = straightforward. **This tells you which service funds the gym and which doesn't.**

**4. Act on what you learn TODAY**
If classes are high-margin but low-utilization, raise prices on unpopular times or bundle them with PT. If PT is low-margin, increase rates (even 10% won't kill bookings). If add-ons are profitable, they should be pushed harder at sign-up and between sets.

**5. Set a monthly review rhythm**
Update numbers the 5th of each month (10 minutes). Watch trends — don't chase one-month flukes. This becomes your single

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automation Stack — Here's The Actual Work

• **Do a live trace RIGHT NOW**: Pick your last 5 enquiries. Manually follow them through every automation touchpoint (email, SMS, calendar booking, CRM tag, trial confirmation). Document where they lag, duplicate, or drop. This takes 90 mins and shows you what's actually broken vs. what you think is working.

• **Kill the duplicate triggers first**: You likely have multiple automations firing on the same action (e.g., "enquiry received" triggering both a welcome email AND a "trial booking" workflow). In your automation platform (Zapier/Make/native CRM), audit triggers only — delete any automation that duplicates another's output. Flag these ruthlessly.

• **Test the actual bottleneck**: The enquiry-to-trial flow breaks at ONE point for most gyms — usually between "email sent" and "trial actually booked." Check: Are your trial-booking links working? Is your calendar synced? Are automations waiting for manual approval? Identify that one friction point and fix it before touching anything else.

• **Create a simple runsheet**: List every automation in order (enquiry → welcome → upsell → trial booking → confirmation → post-trial follow-up). Include platform, trigger, and output. This isn't pretty — it's your map. One column for "working," one for "duplicate," one for "broken." Takes 2 hours.

• **Test with a fake enquiry TODAY**: Submit a dummy contact through your website right now. Time how long it takes to hit each step. You'll spot the real delays immediately instead of guessing.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation Quick Wins for The Forge

**1. Automate class booking confirmations + follow-ups**
- Use Zapier to connect your booking system (or Google Forms) → ChatGPT API → WhatsApp/Email
- **Concrete setup:** When someone books, auto-send confirmation with class details, parking info, and a pre-written "see you tomorrow" reminder 24hrs before
- **Time saved:** ~3-4 hours/week manually messaging members
- **Cost:** ~£25/month Zapier + minimal API costs

**2. Automate new member onboarding sequence**
- Build a 3-email/SMS sequence triggered by signup: welcome → gym orientation video link → nutrition guide → first-week check-in
- **How:** Use Make.com (simpler than Zapier for this) or your email platform's automation
- **Reality check:** This eliminates Grace or staff manually sending the same 5 messages repeatedly
- **Result:** New members feel valued without manual effort; improves retention

**3. Automate social media content repurposing**
- Use ChatGPT to auto-convert one monthly blog post/success story into 8-10 Instagram captions + LinkedIn posts
- **Set it up:** Write prompt template in Notion, feed content through ChatGPT, schedule via Buffer
- **Time saved:** ~2 hours/week on copywriting
- **Bonus:** Post consistently without thinking about it

**Start with #1 this week.** It's the highest-friction, most repetitive task. You'll see ROI immediately in staff time freed up.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execute Now

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY (not tomorrow).** Go to your hosting provider (GoDaddy, Squarespace, Wix?) and activate HTTPS—it's usually £0-30/year and takes 10 minutes. This stops payment data getting intercepted and kills the "not secure" warning that kills conversions.

• **Audit what data you're actually collecting.** Pull your member sign-up forms, payment pages, and contact forms. You're probably storing names, emails, payment details, and maybe health info. Write down what you have—this determines what security rules you must follow (UK GDPR = serious fines for breaches).

• **Lock down admin access immediately.** Change ALL passwords (website login, email, hosting panel). Force strong passwords (16+ characters, mixed case). If you use a developer or VA with access, revoke it or set expiration dates. This is where most breaches happen—not hackers, but weak internal access.

• **Add a basic WAF (Web Application Firewall)** like Cloudflare's free tier. It blocks 80% of automated attacks in 5 minutes to set up. Plug it into your domain settings and you're protected from bots hammering your site.

• **Get liability insurance clarity.** Call your business insurer today. Ask if cybersecurity breaches are covered and what documentation they need (shows you're serious; also protects you if something *does* happen).

**Timeline: 2-3 hours this week. ROI: Massive for member trust + legal compliance.**

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

- Your cost-per-lead has exploded 4.5x from April (£6) to July (£27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% to just £109. This isn't budget constraint—this is a creative and targeting death spiral. The ads that worked in April are burnt out.
- Close rate is actually *up* (75% in July vs 59% average Apr–Jun), so your product and sales process are solid. The problem is volume and efficiency, not conversion. You're getting the *right* people, just far too few.
- July's £109 spend is a skeleton crew budget—too low to gather meaningful data or scale anything. You've under-invested, likely in response to rising costs, which has made the problem worse.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately refresh creative.** Pull the July ads. Analyse what ran in April (when CPL was £6)—if you still have those creative assets, A/B test them head-to-head against entirely new angles: user-generated content from members, before/afters, "6-week challenge" framing, or pain-point focused (e.g. "No mirrors, no judgement"). Run 3–4 fresh ad variants with 40% of next month's budget.

2. **Expand and recombine audiences.** Your targeting has likely tightened too much (chasing only past converters). Test: lookalike audiences built from your April leads, women 25–45 interested in fitness *or* wellness *or* body confidence (not just gym), and geographic expansion beyond your core Belfast postcode into commutable areas. Start with £30–40/day on each.

3. **Reset budget to £300–350 for August** and run a proper test split: 50% on refreshed creative (new angles), 30% on audience expansion, 20% on retargeting website visitors. At your 75% close rate, even a return to £12–15 CPL would give you 20+ leads and 15 sales—profitable at scale.

4. **Single biggest lever:** Stop the bleed on creative fatigue *now*. July's CPL spike is almost entirely ads exhaustion, not market saturation. Fresh creative +
